The retired West Indies cricketer uses the quadratic equation \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \) to find the optimal number of practice sessions. To maximize the average batting score \( S \), we find the vertex of the parabola where the derivative equals zero. Differentiating \( S \) with respect to \( n \) yields \( dS/dn = 5 - 0.4n \). Setting this derivative to zero gives \( 5 = 0.4n \), which solves to \( n = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions are typically whole numbers, we check integers 12 and 13. For \( n = 12 \), the score is \( 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). For \( n = 13 \), the score is \( 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both values yield the same maximum average batting score of 81.2 runs. The optimal number of sessions is effectively 12 or 13, providing the peak performance metric for the team.

For the second sub-problem, we calculate the win probability at two different practice levels. First, we determine the batting score when \( n = 5 \). Substituting into the equation gives \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 0.2(25) = 70 \). Next, we calculate the score when \( n = 10 \). Substituting gives \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 0.2(100) = 90 \). Now we apply the logistic function \( P = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}} \) to these scores. For \( S = 70 \), the exponent is \( -(7 - 3) = -4 \), so \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 0.9820 \). For \( S = 90 \), the exponent is \( -(9 - 3) = -6 \), so \( P_{10} = 1 / (1 + e^{-6}) \approx 0.9975 \). The change in win probability is
